content.problemStatement:
Great! You Have Successfully Built a **JSON Data Processor** Using Python 🎉

Here are some improvements you can try:


**Improve Data Formatting** – Display student records in a more readable tabular format.
**Add Data Backup** – Implement a feature to create a backup of the JSON file before making changes.
**Sorting Feature** – Allow users to sort student records by name, CGPA, or age.
**Update Student Details** – Add functionality to update existing student records.
**Search Enhancements** – Enable partial matching for names (e.g., searching "John" should return "John Doe").
**Final Updates:**
Ensure all **loading, searching, filtering and deleting** functions work correctly.
Handle edge cases like **invalid inputs, empty records, and missing keys**.
Improve user experience with **clear prompts and structured output**.
Try implementing these enhancements to make your **JSON Data Processor** even better! 🚀
constraints: 0 ≤ number of students in a test case ≤ 1000
CGPA is a real number between 0 and 10 (inclusive)
age is an integer between 1 and 100 (inclusive)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case consists of a single line containing a valid JSON string. The JSON object has a key "students" whose value is an array of student records. Every record is a JSON object that may contain the keys "name" (string), "CGPA" (number) and "age" (integer). Keys can appear in any order and some keys may be missing.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output a table with three columns separated by a single tab character. The first line must be the header:
Name\tCGPA\tAge
For every student record output one line in the same order as they appear in the input array. If a key is missing, print "N/A" in its place. Do not print extra blank lines between test cases.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The JSON contains two student objects. The program prints the header followed by each record in the order they appear.
Two records are printed after the header, preserving input order.

solution.approach: For each test case read the whole line, parse it as JSON and extract the array under the key "students". Iterate over the array preserving the original order. For each student object read the three possible fields; if a field is absent output the placeholder "N/A". Finally print the header and all rows using a tab character as separator. The algorithm is linear in the total number of student objects.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total number of student records across all test cases) because each record is visited once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(maximum number of student records in a single test case) for storing the parsed JSON structure.

code.JavaScript: // Note: The duplicate key was removed; only one JavaScript entry is kept.
